name: N+1 Query Problem
bad_example: for user in users: db.query('select * from orders where user_id=?', user.id)
why_bad: Creates one query per row and degrades linearly with list size
good_example: Load related rows with join, eager loading, DataLoader, or WHERE user_id IN (...)
keywords: n+1 query loop dataloader graph resolver

name: Plain-text or weak password storage
bad_example: password_hash = sha1(password)
why_bad: Fast hashes are trivial to crack after database leak
good_example: Use Argon2id or bcrypt with per-password salt and managed pepper
keywords: password md5 sha1 plain text hash

name: Hardcoded secret
bad_example: const JWT_SECRET = 'prod-secret'
why_bad: Secrets leak through git logs builds and client bundles
good_example: Read secrets from environment injection or secret manager and rotate on exposure
keywords: jwt secret api key credential hardcoded

name: Business logic in controller
bad_example: route.post('/orders', async (req,res)=> { calculateDiscount(); chargeCard(); updateInventory(); })
why_bad: Transport layer becomes hard to test reuse and secure
good_example: Move business rules into service layer and keep controller as validation/mapping boundary
keywords: controller business logic handler service layer

name: Unbounded SELECT star
bad_example: select * from events
why_bad: Can load huge tables consume memory and leak columns
good_example: Select explicit columns with limit cursor and index-aligned filters
keywords: select star no limit query table

name: Internal error exposure
bad_example: return err.stack
why_bad: Leaks file paths SQL details secrets and implementation clues
good_example: Log internal error with correlation id and return sanitized error envelope

name: Synchronous slow external work
bad_example: await sendEmail(); await generatePdf(); await resizeImage();
why_bad: Makes requests slow fragile and hard to retry
good_example: Persist intent and process through a background queue with retries and DLQ
keywords: sync email pdf image external api queue

name: Missing transaction for multi-write
bad_example: createOrder(); decrementStock(); chargeLedger();
why_bad: Partial writes corrupt business state when one step fails
good_example: Wrap related database writes in one transaction or use saga for distributed writes
keywords: transaction multi table partial write consistency

name: Retry without idempotency
bad_example: retry POST /charge until success
why_bad: Can duplicate payments orders or side effects
good_example: Use idempotency keys and retry only safe transient failures with backoff

name: Cache without invalidation
bad_example: cache.set('user:1', user) forever
why_bad: Serves stale mutable data and creates support issues
good_example: Use TTL plus explicit invalidation or versioned keys on writes

name: Cross-tenant data leak
bad_example: db.orders.findMany()
why_bad: Missing tenant scope can expose one customer's data to another
good_example: Require tenant scoped repository methods and database RLS for shared schemas
keywords: tenant leak multi tenant rls scope

name: Unverified webhook
bad_example: process(req.body) without signature check
why_bad: Attackers can forge payment or integration events
good_example: Verify HMAC signature timestamp replay window and dedupe event ID

name: Unbounded retry storm
bad_example: while true retry externalApi.call()
why_bad: Amplifies outages and consumes worker capacity
good_example: Use capped exponential backoff jitter circuit breaker and DLQ
keywords: retry storm infinite retry backoff circuit

name: Missing idempotency on payment
bad_example: create charge on every POST retry
why_bad: Client retries can duplicate financial side effects
good_example: Require idempotency key and store request hash result and status

name: Offset pagination on hot feed
bad_example: limit 20 offset 100000
why_bad: Gets slower with deep pages and can skip records under writes
good_example: Use cursor pagination with stable ordered key
keywords: offset pagination feed cursor large list

name: Logging secrets
bad_example: logger.info(req.headers.authorization)
why_bad: Leaks bearer tokens and credentials into log systems
good_example: Redact authorization cookies passwords and API keys before logging

name: No readiness check
bad_example: container starts accepting traffic before db migration
why_bad: Routes traffic to instances that cannot serve requests
good_example: Expose readiness that checks critical dependencies and migration state

name: Distributed transaction by hope
bad_example: write db then call remote service then write another db
why_bad: Partial failure creates inconsistent state across systems
good_example: Use saga outbox idempotency and reconciliation

name: Leaky ORM entities
bad_example: return UserEntity directly from API
why_bad: Exposes internal columns and couples API to persistence schema
good_example: Map entities to explicit response DTOs

name: No query timeout
bad_example: db.query(reportSql) with default timeout
why_bad: Slow queries can exhaust pools and cascade latency
good_example: Set statement timeout and move expensive reports to async jobs

name: IDOR missing ownership check
bad_example: GET /users/123/invoices returns data without actor ownership check
why_bad: Attackers can access other users resources by changing IDs
good_example: Check object ownership or tenant policy before returning data

name: SQL string concatenation
bad_example: db.query('select * from users where email=' + email)
why_bad: Allows attacker-controlled SQL execution
good_example: Use parameterized queries or safe query builders

name: Mass assignment role update
bad_example: User.update(req.body)
why_bad: Client can set role isAdmin or server-controlled fields
good_example: Map only explicitly allowed DTO fields

name: Wildcard CORS with credentials
bad_example: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and credentials true
why_bad: Allows unintended browser origins to read authenticated responses
good_example: Use explicit origin allowlist and avoid wildcard credentials

name: No CSRF protection on cookie auth
bad_example: POST /transfer relies only on session cookie
why_bad: Cross-site requests can trigger authenticated writes
good_example: Use SameSite plus CSRF tokens for unsafe methods

name: SSRF through URL fetch
bad_example: fetch(req.body.url) from backend
why_bad: Attacker can reach internal metadata or private services
good_example: Validate URL resolve IP and block private networks

name: File upload trust
bad_example: store uploaded file and serve it directly
why_bad: Malicious files can execute or expose users
good_example: Validate type scan content and serve from isolated storage

name: JWT none or weak algorithm
bad_example: accept token alg from header without allowlist
why_bad: Attackers can bypass signature verification
good_example: Allowlist expected algorithm and key only

name: Refresh token reuse ignored
bad_example: issue new access token even after old refresh token reused
why_bad: Stolen refresh tokens remain useful
good_example: Rotate refresh tokens and revoke token family on reuse

name: No password rehash path
bad_example: keep bcrypt cost from 2016 forever
why_bad: Password security does not improve with hardware changes
good_example: Rehash on login when algorithm parameters are outdated

name: External call inside DB transaction
bad_example: begin transaction then call payment API before commit
why_bad: Long locks and partial failures harm consistency
good_example: Commit local intent then use outbox or saga for external call

name: Long-running report in request
bad_example: GET /report computes millions of rows synchronously
why_bad: Requests time out and compete with normal traffic
good_example: Move report to async job with progress and download

name: Unbounded worker concurrency
bad_example: start worker with concurrency 1000
why_bad: Can overload database or downstream services
good_example: Set concurrency based on dependency capacity and queue age

name: No DLQ ownership
bad_example: failed jobs go to DLQ nobody monitors
why_bad: Failures become silent data loss
good_example: Alert on DLQ and assign replay ownership

name: Cache authorization decision forever
bad_example: cache allow admin forever
why_bad: Permission changes do not take effect
good_example: Use short TTL and invalidate on role or policy changes

name: PII in analytics event
bad_example: track('signup', { email, phone, ssn })
why_bad: Sensitive data spreads to analytics systems
good_example: Send minimal identifiers and classify event fields

name: PII in URL query string
bad_example: GET /reset?token=secret
why_bad: URLs leak through logs browser history and referrers
good_example: Send secrets in body or one-time opaque flow

name: No audit for admin action
bad_example: admin changes user role without audit record
why_bad: Investigations cannot reconstruct sensitive changes
good_example: Write audit event with actor target action and reason

name: Shared admin account
bad_example: team uses one admin login
why_bad: No accountability and weak offboarding
good_example: Use individual accounts with MFA and role-based access

name: No tenant test coverage
bad_example: repository methods are untested for tenant scoping
why_bad: Cross-tenant bugs can ship unnoticed
good_example: Add tests proving tenant A cannot access tenant B data

name: Leaky feature flag
bad_example: if beta then skip auth check
why_bad: Flags accidentally bypass security or validation
good_example: Keep auth outside flag branches and test both states

name: In-memory session only
bad_example: sessions stored in process memory on multi-instance app
why_bad: Users are logged out or routed inconsistently across instances
good_example: Use shared session store or stateless token with revocation strategy

name: Serverless connection storm
bad_example: new database pool per invocation without proxy
why_bad: Database connection limits are exhausted under burst
good_example: Use connection proxy and reuse clients where runtime allows

name: No schema migration rollback plan
bad_example: deploy migration with no tested recovery
why_bad: Failed migration can prolong outage
good_example: Use backward-compatible migrations and tested restore or rollback plan

name: Unsafe deserialization
bad_example: deserialize user supplied object into runtime type
why_bad: Can trigger remote code execution or object injection
good_example: Use safe formats and explicit schemas

name: Overbroad service token
bad_example: integration token has full admin access
why_bad: Compromise gives attacker excessive permissions
good_example: Issue scoped tokens per integration and rotate them

name: No rate limit on login
bad_example: POST /login unlimited attempts
why_bad: Enables brute force and credential stuffing
good_example: Rate limit by account IP and device with abuse monitoring

name: Silent degraded mode
bad_example: fallback used for days with no alert
why_bad: Users get stale or reduced results without operator awareness
good_example: Emit metric and alert when fallback is active

name: No clock tolerance for webhook
bad_example: reject all webhooks if server clock drifts
why_bad: Valid events fail during clock skew
good_example: Use NTP and a bounded timestamp tolerance

name: Raw exception in queue consumer
bad_example: consumer crashes and drops message after exception
why_bad: Messages can be lost or retried without context
good_example: Catch classify retry or DLQ with structured error context
